question:
How many primes among the positive integers, written as usual in base 10,
are alternating 1's and 0's, beginning and ending with 1?
solution:
Solution. Suppose that \( N=101 \cdots 0101 \) with \( k \) ones, for some \( k \geq 2 \). Then
\[
99 N=9999 \cdots 9999=10^{2 k}-1=\left(10^{k}+1\right)\left(10^{k}-1\right) .
\]

If moreover \( N \) is prime, then \( N \) divides either \( 10^{k}+1 \) or \( 10^{k}-1 \), and hence one of \( \frac{99}{10^{k}-1}=\frac{10^{k}+1}{N} \) and \( \frac{99}{10^{k}+1}=\frac{10^{k}-1}{N} \) is an integer. For \( k>2,10^{k}-1 \) and \( 10^{k}+1 \) are both greater than 99 , so we get a contradiction. Therefore \( k=2 \) and \( N=101 \) (which is prime).

Remark. Essentially the same problem appeared on the 1979 British Mathematical Olympiad, reprinted in [Lar1, p. 123] as Problem 4.1.4:

Prove that there are no prime numbers in the infinite sequence of integers
\[
10001,100010001,1000100010001, \ldots .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
How many primes among the positive integers, written as usual in base 10,
are alternating 1's and 0's, beginning and ending with 1?
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Suppose that \( integern=101 \cdots 0101 \) with \( onescount \) ones, for some \( onescount \geq 2 \). Then
\[
99\, integern=9999 \cdots 9999=10^{2\,onescount}-1=\left(10^{onescount}+1\right)\left(10^{onescount}-1\right) .
\]

If moreover \( integern \) is prime, then \( integern \) divides either \( 10^{onescount}+1 \) or \( 10^{onescount}-1 \), and hence one of \( \frac{99}{10^{onescount}-1}=\frac{10^{onescount}+1}{integern} \) and \( \frac{99}{10^{onescount}+1}=\frac{10^{onescount}-1}{integern} \) is an integer. For \( onescount>2,10^{onescount}-1 \) and \( 10^{onescount}+1 \) are both greater than 99 , so we get a contradiction. Therefore \( onescount=2 \) and \( integern=101 \) (which is prime).

Remark. Essentially the same problem appeared on the 1979 British Mathematical Olympiad, reprinted in [Lar1, p. 123] as Problem 4.1.4:

Prove that there are no prime numbers in the infinite sequence of integers
\[
10001,100010001,1000100010001, \ldots .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
How many primes among the positive integers, written as usual in base 10,
are alternating 1's and 0's, beginning and ending with 1?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Suppose that \( longitude=101 \cdots 0101 \) with \( backpack \) ones, for some \( backpack \geq 2 \). Then
\[
99 longitude=9999 \cdots 9999=10^{2 backpack}-1=\left(10^{backpack}+1\right)\left(10^{backpack}-1\right) .
\]

If moreover \( longitude \) is prime, then \( longitude \) divides either \( 10^{backpack}+1 \) or \( 10^{backpack}-1 \), and hence one of \( \frac{99}{10^{backpack}-1}=\frac{10^{backpack}+1}{longitude} \) and \( \frac{99}{10^{backpack}+1}=\frac{10^{backpack}-1}{longitude} \) is an integer. For \( backpack>2,10^{backpack}-1 \) and \( 10^{backpack}+1 \) are both greater than 99 , so we get a contradiction. Therefore \( backpack=2 \) and \( longitude=101 \) (which is prime).

Remark. Essentially the same problem appeared on the 1979 British Mathematical Olympiad, reprinted in [Lar1, p. 123] as Problem 4.1.4:

Prove that there are no prime numbers in the infinite sequence of integers
\[
10001,100010001,1000100010001, \ldots .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
How many primes among the positive integers, written as usual in base 10,
are alternating 1's and 0's, beginning and ending with 1?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Suppose that \( nonnumber=101 \cdots 0101 \) with \( infinitude \) ones, for some \( infinitude \geq 2 \). Then
\[
99 nonnumber=9999 \cdots 9999=10^{2 infinitude}-1=\left(10^{infinitude}+1\right)\left(10^{infinitude}-1\right) .
\]

If moreover \( nonnumber \) is prime, then \( nonnumber \) divides either \( 10^{infinitude}+1 \) or \( 10^{infinitude}-1 \), and hence one of \( \frac{99}{10^{infinitude}-1}=\frac{10^{infinitude}+1}{nonnumber} \) and \( \frac{99}{10^{infinitude}+1}=\frac{10^{infinitude}-1}{nonnumber} \) is an integer. For \( infinitude>2,10^{infinitude}-1 \) and \( 10^{infinitude}+1 \) are both greater than 99 , so we get a contradiction. Therefore \( infinitude=2 \) and \( nonnumber=101 \) (which is prime).

Remark. Essentially the same problem appeared on the 1979 British Mathematical Olympiad, reprinted in [Lar1, p. 123] as Problem 4.1.4:

Prove that there are no prime numbers in the infinite sequence of integers
\[
10001,100010001,1000100010001, \ldots .
\]

garbled_string.question:
How many primes among the positive integers, written as usual in base 10,
are alternating 1's and 0's, beginning and ending with 1?
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Suppose that \( qzxwvtnp=101 \cdots 0101 \) with \( hjgrksla \) ones, for some \( hjgrksla \geq 2 \). Then
\[
99 qzxwvtnp=9999 \cdots 9999=10^{2 hjgrksla}-1=\left(10^{hjgrksla}+1\right)\left(10^{hjgrksla}-1\right) .
\]

If moreover \( qzxwvtnp \) is prime, then \( qzxwvtnp \) divides either \( 10^{hjgrksla}+1 \) or \( 10^{hjgrksla}-1 \), and hence one of \( \frac{99}{10^{hjgrksla}-1}=\frac{10^{hjgrksla}+1}{qzxwvtnp} \) and \( \frac{99}{10^{hjgrksla}+1}=\frac{10^{hjgrksla}-1}{qzxwvtnp} \) is an integer. For \( hjgrksla>2,10^{hjgrksla}-1 \) and \( 10^{hjgrksla}+1 \) are both greater than 99 , so we get a contradiction. Therefore \( hjgrksla=2 \) and \( qzxwvtnp=101 \) (which is prime).

Remark. Essentially the same problem appeared on the 1979 British Mathematical Olympiad, reprinted in [Lar1, p. 123] as Problem 4.1.4:

Prove that there are no prime numbers in the infinite sequence of integers
\[
10001,100010001,1000100010001, \ldots .
\]

kernel_variant.question: How many prime numbers have a base-6 representation that consists of alternating digits 1 and 0, beginning and ending with 1, and that contains at least three base-6 digits?  (Typical examples of such numerals are 101_6, 10101_6, 1010101_6, \ldots .)
kernel_variant.solution:
Write such a number with k \geq  2 occurrences of the digit 1:

        N_k = 1010\ldots 01 (k ones and k-1 zeros)_6.

In base-10 this equals the geometric sum

        N_k = 6^{2k-2} + 6^{2k-4} + \cdots  + 6^{2} + 1 = \Sigma _{j=0}^{k-1} 6^{2j}
             = (6^{2k} - 1)/(6^{2} - 1) = (6^{2k} - 1)/35.

Multiplying both sides by 35 gives a repunit in base 6:

        35 N_k = 6^{2k} - 1 = (6^{k} - 1)(6^{k} + 1).               (1)

Because gcd(6^{k} - 1, 6^{k} + 1) = 1, a prime factor can divide at most one of the two factors on the right.  Hence a prime N_k must satisfy exactly one of the divisibilities

        N_k | (6^{k} - 1) or N_k | (6^{k} + 1).

Cancelling N_k from (1) in each case gives

        (i)   N_k | (6^{k} - 1)  \Rightarrow   6^{k} + 1 | 35,             or
        (ii)  N_k | (6^{k} + 1)  \Rightarrow   6^{k} - 1 | 35.              (2)

All positive divisors of 35 are 1, 5, 7, 35.  We examine (2) case by case.

Case (i)  6^{k} + 1 divides 35.
   The only possibilities are 1, 5, 7, 35, all of which are < 6^{2}.  But 6^{k} + 1 \geq  6^{2} + 1 = 37 for every k \geq  2, so no value of k satisfies (i).

Case (ii)  6^{k} - 1 divides 35.
   Again 6^{k} - 1 can be 1, 5, 7, or 35.
        6^{k} - 1 = 1  \Rightarrow  k = 0   (not allowed)
        6^{k} - 1 = 5  \Rightarrow  6^{k} = 6  \Rightarrow  k = 1  (not allowed)
        6^{k} - 1 = 7  \Rightarrow  6^{k} = 8  (impossible)
        6^{k} - 1 = 35 \Rightarrow  6^{k} = 36 \Rightarrow  k = 2, which is admissible.
Thus the only possible value is k = 2.

For k = 2,
        N_2 = 6^{2} + 1 = 37,
which is prime.

Consequently, exactly one prime possesses the stated form.

Answer: 1.

core_steps: Express N with k alternating digits and note that (10²−1)·N = 10^{2k} − 1
Factor 10^{2k} − 1 as (10^{k} − 1)(10^{k} + 1) via difference of squares
Because N is prime, N must divide exactly one of those two factors
Compare sizes: for k>2 both factors exceed (10²−1), contradicting divisibility by N
Conclude k=2 and verify the lone remaining candidate for primality

slot1.description: The base in which the digits are written

slot2.description: The non–zero digit that alternates with zeros (now fixed at 1)

slot3.description: Multiplier equal to base²−1 that turns the pattern into a repunit of (base−1)’s

slot4.description: Numerical threshold used to bound k (same number as slot3 in this instance)

slot5.description: The final candidate when k=2 (here equal to base²+1)
